context:
This course explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn to make strategic decisions related to supply chain network design, supply chain finance, supplier management and procurement, production and material requirements planning, distribution, Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP), and process and organizational design. By the end of the course you will be equipped to optimize supply chain networks, apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions, and lead cross-functional initiatives that balance operational excellence with strategic business objectives. This course is part of the MITx MicroMasters® Program in Supply Chain Management.

Syllabus:
CTL.SC2x Supply Chain Design

Program:
Part of the MITx MicroMasters Program in Supply Chain Management.

Course Description:
Supply Chain Design: SC2x explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn how to make strategic decisions related to network design, supply chain finance, supplier management, production planning, distribution, and organizational design.

Learning Objectives:
- Design and optimize supply chain networks
- Apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions
- Develop supplier management and procurement strategies
- Plan production and material requirements
- Coordinate Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP) processes
- Improve cross-functional communication and organizational performance

Prerequisites:
There are no formal prerequisites. However, because SC2x is a quantitative course, learners benefit from familiarity with algebra, spreadsheets, and introductory concepts in statistics, probability, and optimization. Learners seeking additional preparation may wish to complete Supply Chain Analytics: SC0x and Supply Chain Fundamentals: SC1x first.

Estimated Effort:
8–12 hours per week for 12 weeks.

Intended Audience:
Professionals and lifelong learners interested in supply chain management, operations, logistics, and business analytics.

Course Schedule:
New material for each week is released on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC. Graded assignments are due on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC on the specified deadline.
- Week 0 — Course Overview & Logistics (starts Jun 17, 2026)
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design (Jun 24 – Jul 8, 2026)
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design (Jul 1 – Jul 15, 2026)
- Week 3 — Production Planning (Jul 8 – Jul 22, 2026)
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies (Jul 15 – Jul 29, 2026)
- Week 5 — Prep Week (Jul 22, 2026) — no graded assignment; review for the Midterm
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam (Jul 29 – Jul 31, 2026)
- Week 7 — Supplier Management (Aug 5 – Aug 19, 2026)
- Week 8 — Supply Chain Finance I (Aug 12 – Aug 26, 2026)
- Week 9 — Supply Chain Finance II (Aug 19 – Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 10 — Supply Chain Finance III and Organizational Design (Aug 26 – Sep 9, 2026)
- Week 11 — Prep Week (Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 12 — Final Exam (Sep 9 – Sep 11, 2026)

Key Dates:
Course starts Jun 17, 2026; Financial assistance application deadline Jul 8, 2026; Enrollment / Verification upgrade deadline (Certificate Track) Jul 22, 2026; Course ends Sep 11, 2026; Certificates available Sep 18, 2026.

Weekly Topics:
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design: Introduces supply chain design and reviews key concepts from SC1x. Formulate and solve network flow models using spreadsheets, then explore the Facility Location Problem, including its formulation and solution as a Mixed Integer Linear Program (MILP).
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design: Combine the Facility Location problem with network flow models to formulate and solve larger network design problems including facility capacity, level of service, and inbound/outbound transportation. Cover modeling multiple products, echelons, and periods; discuss flexibility and robustness.
- Week 3 — Production Planning: Move from network design to working with internal and external manufacturing partners. Present the Fixed Planning Horizon model; introduce Bill of Materials (BOM), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), and Distribution Requirements Planning (DRP) systems.
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies: The S&OP process is presented and discussed, along with different distribution strategies and how e-commerce has affected the design of distribution networks.
- Week 5 — Prep Week: Catch up and review; addresses practical concerns of running a network design project, in preparation for the Midterm.
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam: Assesses understanding of concepts and methods covered in Weeks 1–4.
- Weeks 7–10 — Supplier Management and Supply Chain Finance: Procurement strategy and optimization-based procurement; accounting fundamentals and cost accounting; cash flows and discounted cash flows; supply chain finance, financial performance, and organizational/metric design.
- Week 12 — Final Exam.

Assessment & Grading:
The course separates a learning space (videos, quick questions, and practice problems with immediate feedback; weekly Graded Assignments as formative assessments, with solutions released after the due date) from an assessment space (the Midterm and Final Exams, which evaluate individual mastery; no solutions or individualized feedback are provided for exam questions). To pass the course and earn a Certificate, you must achieve a final course grade of at least 60%. Grade weights:
- Weekly Graded Assignments: 20%
- Midterm Exam: 35%
- Final Exam: 45%

Enrollment Track Access:
Graded Assignments for Weeks 1, 2, and 3 are available to both Audit and Certificate Track learners. Graded Assignments for Weeks 4, 7, 8, 9, and 10, the Midterm Exam, and the Final Exam are available only to Certificate Track learners.

Graded Assignments are released with each content week and may be completed any time before the deadline (deadlines are firm; no extensions). Point values are indicated per question; most questions allow two submission attempts; only the last submission is graded; you must click Submit for each answer. A green/red indicator shows correctness after submission, and official solutions and explanations are released after the deadline. Assignments remain available for review afterward.

Exams: the Midterm and Final together account for 80% of the final grade. They are open-book (you may consult course videos, slides, Key Concept Documents, and your own notes) and timed. Each exam is available during a 48-hour testing window; once started you have 2.5 hours, and the timer cannot be paused, stopped, or reset (start at least 2.5 hours before the deadline for full time). The Discussion Forum and Progress Tab are disabled while an exam is open. Unlike Graded Assignments, exams provide no correctness feedback and no solutions after the deadline. Timed exams must be completed in a web browser.

Policies & Support:
Academic integrity: All graded work must comply with MIT Learn's Honor Code and Academic Integrity Policy — original work; no unauthorized collaboration; no use of generative AI tools unless explicitly permitted by the teaching team; a single account; fair competition; and protecting assessment integrity (do not post, share, or distribute answers). Violations may result in invalidation of graded work, certificate revocation, or removal from the course.
Communication: The Discussion Forum (monitored by Community Teaching Assistants and staff) is for course concepts, videos, quick questions, practice problems, and general problem-solving approaches; do not use it for Weekly Graded Assignments, the Midterm, or the Final. Assessment-related questions go to the email help desk at sc2xhelp@mit.edu (include the week, problem number, and a description); staff will not review calculations, verify answers, or provide hints for graded work.
Accessibility: For disability-related accommodations, contact mitx-accessibility@mit.edu as early as possible (at least two weeks before an exam opens for exam accommodations).

Course Team:
Instructors: Dr. Chris Caplice; Prof. Yossi Sheffi; Dr. Jarrod Goentzel; Mr. Jim Rice. Course Lead: Ms. Laura Allegue. Teaching Assistant: Mr. Matt Toy. MicroMasters in SCM Team: Dr. Eva Ponce (Director); Dr. Inma Borrella (Academic Lead); Ms. Cara Greaney; Mr. Kellen Betts; Mr. Paulo de Sousa; Mr. Jeff Baker; Mr. Robert Cummings; Mr. Connor Makowski; Ms. Elise Yonika. Course help / assessment questions: sc2xhelp@mit.edu

TITLE: Week 2 Practice Problems — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design

Ungraded practice problems for Week 2. Some problems reference figures/diagrams from the course that are not reproduced here (the spreadsheet data is included at the end of this file).

Practice Problem 1: Estimating costs
After hearing about your skills in building models for supply chain decision support, a small widget manufacturer has asked for your help in planning their distribution. Currently, the firm runs a small network with three (3) manufacturing sites, one (1) DC, and eight (8) regional warehouses. The widgets are transported by a dedicated carrier from the manufacturing sites through the DC to the regional warehouses.
In particular, the manufacturer has asked for your help in deciding how many DCs to use in the network, as well as which potential DC locations to use. Having taken a course in Supply Chain Design, you quickly understand that before you can help the manufacturer modeling the problem, you need to understand current costs.
After talking to the manufacturer, the manufacturer sends you the following data-file: data (Excel); data (LibreOffice).
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
Please refer to the data file, tab "Inbound Transportation". This data shows the shipment volume and the price paid for the shipment to the carrier for a number of shipments from the different manufacturing sites to the DC.
Based on your estimates, what are the manufacturer's transportation costs per widget for the three arcs? Answer in dollars without decimals, e.g., if your solution is $4.25, write 4. In this case, transport cost is the price paid to the carrier.

0 points possible (ungraded)
When you report your findings to the widget manufacturer, they tell you that the transportation costs are not linear in the number of widgets per shipment. Instead, they tell you, they pay a minimum cost per shipment independent of volume. When you look at the data again you realize that this can actually be seen in the data.
When you build a supply chain network optimization model based on your estimated variable transportation costs, what type of bias will you have to be aware of?
Select the best answer

Practice Problem 2: Papper
Papper is a paper goods manufacturer. One of their main paper products is a paper towel, which is produced in two (2) plants in the contiguous U.S. From the plants, the paper towels are sent to nine (9) regional warehouses (RW), from where they are then shipped to customers across the U.S.
The distribution manager at Papper wants to reduce the number of direct transports in the network, reduce the inventory levels at the plants, and move inventory closer to the regional warehouses, by introducing a central warehouse (CW) where all finished paper towels are stored before further shipment. That is, the idea is to have the towels flowing from the plants to the CW and then to the RWs. The CW will have a small safety stock, but everything that comes into the CW during a week should also leave the CW during that week.
The distribution manager has found five (5) candidate locations for the CW that he is now choosing between. Capacity is not an issue at any of them.
All data, including weekly demand data (in number of boxes), can be downloaded in (Excel) or in (Libre Office). Assume transportation cost is $1 per mile per box of paper towels.
Hint - Sanity Check:  You need to build a supply chain network model in your spreadsheet, following the steps of the lessons. To check your spreadsheet, try setting each decision variable (the flow on each of the 10 inbound arcs, 45 outbound arcs, and the five binary open-or-not variables) = 1. Also, for this sanity check to work, note that you should link your variable costs at the CWs to the units outflow to the RWs. Using the units inflow would also work for the optimized solution because of the conservation of flow constraint but will lead you to a different answer at this non-optimized step. With that all set and without running the solver, your total cost should be $405,282. If this is not the case, check your model!
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
Out of the candidate locations, which central warehouse location minimizes total costs (while meeting the constraints)?
Select the best answer
CW 1
CW 2
CW 3
CW 4
CW 5
Part 2
0 points possible (ungraded)
Suppose the distribution manager wants at least 60% of regional demand to be within 70 miles of the central warehouse(s).
How many central warehouses are used in the optimal solution, if we introduce the constraint that 60% of regional demand must be within 70 miles of a central warehouse?
Select the best answer

0 points possible (ungraded)
Suppose the same service constraints as in Part 2 applies.
After investigating the transport market, the manager realizes that he has completely overestimated the inbound transportation costs. In fact, inbound transportation costs can be reduced to $0.3 per mile and box.
How does this change the optimal network design (compared to Part 2)?
Select the best answer
It is now optimal to use Central Warehouse 4
The amount of demand within 70 miles decreases
More production is allocated to Plant 1
Manufacturing costs increase
All of the above

Practice Problem 3: Colombian Premium Coffee
A traditional Colombian Coffee manufacturer called CPC has hired your services as a supply chain consultor to analyze the distribution of its brand-new premium product, named Coffee Indeed ®. This product is 1 package containing 1-kilogram of ground and roasted Arabica coffee to be commercialized and distributed in three cities: Bogotá (DC 1), Medellín (DC 2), and Cali (DC 3). Due to governmental tax benefits, the company roasts and packs the product in two factories: Barranquilla (Plant 1) and Santander (Plant 2). CPC hires carriers to transport the products from the plants to each of the three distribution centers (DCs). Each DC has a different demand, and each plant has a different production capacity.
Demand:
Bogotá: 345,000 units per week
Medellín: 263,000 units per week
Cali: 172,000 units per week
Plant Capacities:
Barranquilla: 610,000 units per week
Santander: 350,000 units per week
Part 0
0 points possible (ungraded)
This question works as a checkpoint to support you in building your model.
Have in mind that this problem does not constrain the number of pallets to be an integer; therefore, you can use the number of pallets with decimal places.
Under the same assumption, the price per pallet is proportional to the number of units it's carrying, so you can analyze the transportation cost in the same units that Demand and Capacity are expressed. We know this is not always the case in real life but think of this as a simplified model.
Please, make your model and set all decision variables into "1".
A) What is the capacity constraint of Barranquilla (answer in number of units)
B) What is the total number of units of products transported from Barranquilla (Plant 1) to all DCs?
C) What is the total number of units of product transported from all plants to Medellín (DC 2)?
D) What is the total demand of Medellín (DC 2) in units?
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)

Both plants can produce both products: the 1-kilogram Coffee Indeed ® (product 1) and the 500-grams Coffee Indeed ® (product 2). Capacity in units applies to any product unit, independently of its weight or packaging. This new product 2 is transported in the same vehicles of product 1 using the same transportation costs; however, 1 pallet can hold 2000 units (two thousand) of product 2.

Practice Problem 4: Harley Stevenson
HarleyStevenson is a bicycle manufacturer operating a small supply chain distribution network. After hearing about your expertise in Supply Chain Network Design, they have asked you to help them analyze their network to find opportunities for improvement.
All HarleyStevenson's bicycles are made to stock from a single manufacturing site. Once per week, finished bicycles are transported in large batches, using direct transports, from the manufacturing site to three (3) Distribution Centers (DCs). The DCs are all operated by third party providers, at the following weekly costs for HarleyStevenson:
DC1: Fixed cost: $2,000, Variable cost: $5 per bicycle
DC2: Fixed cost: $1,700, Variable cost: $6 per bicycle
DC3: Fixed cost: $1,700, Variable cost: $5 per bicycle
From the DCs, the bicycles are transported to five (5) sales regions using common carrier transport. Current company policy states that each sales region be supplied by only one (1) of the DCs, but in practice this is not always the case. Transport cost data as well as the actual flows for a few typical weeks are found in the following data file: Excel, LibreOffice.
(Note from the data that the inbound transports may differ in price per bicycle each week, whereas the outbound transports are paid for according to the common carrier's list price, specified per bicycle.)
There are currently no capacity restrictions.
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
To analyze HarleyStevenson's network in an optimization model, you realize you need to understand the transportation costs. You decide to make a linear approximation for the inbound transportation costs.
Please refer to the data file, tab 'Inbound Transportation'. What is the average inbound transportation cost per bicycle for the three DCs? Answer in dollars without the dollar sign, e.g., if your solution is $23.45, write 23.
Note: When you check your answer you will check all three boxes below!

DC2

Practice Problem 6: TIme-Expanded Network
In this problem, we will consider the following formulation of a time-expanded network problem.
Min \( z = \sum_{i} \sum_{j} \sum_{t} c_{ijt} x_{ijt} + \sum_{j} \sum_{t} h I_{jt} \)
s.t.
\( \sum_{j} x_{ijt} \leq S_{it} \quad \forall i \in S, t \in T \)
\( \sum_{i} x_{ijt} \geq D_{jt} \quad \forall j \in D, t \in T \)
\( \sum_{i} x_{ijt} - \sum_{i} x_{jit} + I_{j,t-1} - I_{jt} = 0 \quad \forall j \notin S, t \in T \)
\( I_{jt} = I_{jt}^{initial} \quad \forall j, t = 0 \)
\( x_{ijt} \geq 0 \quad \forall i,j,t \)
Part 1
0 points possible (ungraded)
In time-expanded models, we investigate the system across multiple time periods. This approach allows products within the model to move not just spatially but also temporally. Additionally, it permits products to move solely through time, enabling them to remain at the same network node across several periods.
Contrary to the standard transport or transshipment models, time-expanded models allow for explicit incorporation of inventory holding. In the formulation mentioned, \(h\) represents the inventory holding cost.
To include inventory holding costs in our model, which of the following costs are typically considered part of the holding cost at facility \(i\)?
Select all correct answers:
Transportation cost to facility \(i\) from all other facilities
Facility overhead
Insurance

Cost of capital

Relevant lecture material (retrieved from the course lectures):

[local:lecture_00_04_key_concept_document]
The objective is to minimize costs for a
given period (day, week, year) by choosing the number of units to be shipped/transported on diﬀerent
arcs during the period to fulﬁll demand, while meeting the capacity constraints. Both problems can be
formulated as linear programs (see below) and solved using your spreadsheet software of choice (e.g. Excel
or Libre Oﬃce). For details on how to run the solver, please refer to the separate guide for how to setup
and run the solver, which is found in the course material. We discuss some of the limitations to these network models. First, in the models review, we limited
ourselves to variable costs for the arcs (i.e. transport costs per unit). Clearly, the cost structure for a
network is, in practice, often more complex, involving costs that are both ﬁxed or vary over diﬀerent
parameters. We also considered a single commodity – all plants produced perfectly substitutable goods. The demand was deterministic, that is, we assumed that demand was perfectly known for the entire
period. Finally, we also assumed there was no capacity limits on the arcs, so that any amount could be
transported. Clearly, all of these assumptions limit our results, so we have to be careful what inferences
we make from the models. In the coming lessons we will relax some of these and also discuss how to make
inference while still using a relatively simple model. Also – we noted that transportation and transshipment problems may have several optima (the same
value of the objective function is found by diﬀerent value on the decision variables). Depending on the algorithm and software, diﬀerent optima may be the “ﬁrst choice” of the algorithm. Network terminology
• Node or vertices – a point (facility, DC, plant, region)
• Arc or edge – link between two nodes (roads, ﬂows, etc.)
• Network or graph – a collection of nodes and arcs
The transportation problem
The transportation problem considers transports from i supply nodes to j demand nodes over arcs ij. With each arc is associated a cost cij. The number of units transported on each arc ij is denoted xij. These xij’s are our decision variables – we want to ﬁnd the amounts for each arc that minimizes total
cost. Let z be the objective function (i.e., the function expressing the total cost we want to minimize).

[local:lecture_00_04_key_concept_document]
Executives and managers have additional
experience and insights into the larger environment that need to be considered when making large and
important decisions, like supply chain network design. Learning Objectives
• Introduce network models
• Review how formulate the transportation problem and the transshipment problem
• Learn how to formulate the continuous single facility problem
• Formulate and solve network facility location problems (discrete candidate selection)
• Use network ﬂow models to evaluate the number of facilities in a network
• Incorporate Level-Of-Service (LOS) constraints in the network models
• Identify how to combine previous models to a Supply Chain Network Design Model
• Construct relevant baselines for the analysis
• Perform robust optimization using supply network models
Supply Chain Design 143
• Construct multi-commodity ﬂow models
• Review how to expand the model into several time periods
• Understand when to incorporate inventory costs into the network models
• Gain an appreciation and understanding of practical considerations when conducting SCND engage-
ments
References
• Watson, Michael, Sara Lewis, Peter Cacioppi, and Jay Jayaraman, Supply Chain Network Design, 1st
Edition, FT Press, 2013
• Chopra, Sunil and Peter Meindl, Supply Chain Management, Strategy, Planning, and Operation, 5th
edition, Pearson Prentice Hall, 2012. • Ellet, Steve, Conversations and Presentations, Chainalytics, 2005-2015. • Jordan and Graves (1995), “Principles on the Beneﬁts of Manufacturing Process Flexibility,” Manage-
ment Science, 41, (4), p 577-594. • Rogers, Paul and Marcia Blenko, “Who Has the D?: How Clear Decision Roles Enhance Organizational
Performance,” Harvard Business Review, January 2006. • Shapiro, Jeremy. Modeling the Supply Chain, 2nd Edition, Duxbury Applied Series, 2007. 3.3 Production Planning
3.3.1 Summary
We now review the information ﬂow within a ﬁrm, and the coordination and planning of production. What is being coordinated? Well, what we want to achieve is to match the demand of our customers with
the supply from our ﬁrm, which requires components from our suppliers. For this match to be possible,
information about future demand should be used as an input of the master production schedule, which
is then used to determine the materials requirements.

[local:lecture_00_04_key_concept_document]
We take network optimization and
combine it with the facility location models together into what’s known as a supply chain network design
model. Data collection: Transportation Data
The ﬁrst step in building the model is to collect data. For transports, we want to know the costs and
capacities associated with both inbound and outbound transportation. With the Supply Chain Network Design Model, transportation costs are assumed linear in transport
volumes. Clearly, this is not always the case: there may be minimum charges, ﬁxed costs, or many other
types of ﬁxed and variable cost components. However, in order to build the model, a linear approximation
must be found. This could be done in several diﬀerent ways:
• Take average transport cost from historical data
• Use list prices
• Use regression analysis to ﬁnd the costs
• Use benchmark rates from other sources
Whichever way you use to uncover these costs, you need to be aware of how your linear approximation
aﬀects the reliability of the model. Data collection: Facility Data
The Supply Chain Network Design Model allows facility costs to be both ﬁxed and variable in volume. There will always be people arguing that “all costs are ﬁxed” or “all costs are variable”, but what you
need to consider is how ﬁxed and variable cost components aﬀect the solution your model searches for . Fixed costs will make it expensive to have many facilities, so the model will try to reduce the number
138 MITx MicroMasters Program in SCM Key Concepts
of facilities. Variable costs will make it costly to have long distances, and the model will therefore try to
increase the number of facilities (or at least reduce average distances). If facilities are owned and/or operated by a third-party, ﬁnding the ﬁxed and variable costs is normally
straight-forward – they are given in the contract. If a facility is operated in-house, other techniques have
to be used. Among them:
• Activity-based costing to ﬁnd variable costs
• Regression analysis over volume (note that the intercept is the ﬁxed cost component)
For facilities we also need to understand the capacity. The capacity is the maximum throughput over
a speciﬁed unit of time (e.g. a week). Note that this can be very diﬃcult to measure in practice, since
shifts can be added, while capacity also depends on planning and scheduling.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on the Week 2 practice stuff for advanced supply chain network design. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to help you work through it! There's quite a bit covered in Week 2, so let's make sure we focus on what's actually tripping you up.

Which practice problem are you working on right now, and what specifically is confusing you about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion and asking for a broad explanation, which would amount to doing the work for them. Instead, I should narrow the scope by asking them to identify which specific practice problem or concept is giving them trouble. This respects the Socratic approach and keeps the conversation productive without front-loading information the student hasn't asked to discover themselves.

exchanges.student: Uh, kind of all of it? Like facility location, flow balance, fixed vs variable costs, even how I’m supposed to read these spreadsheets. I feel like if I only ask about one part I’m still going to miss the big picture.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — it can feel overwhelming when everything seems connected. But here's the thing: the big picture actually *emerges* from working through the pieces, not the other way around.

So let's pick a starting point. Which practice problem feels most approachable to you right now, even if just slightly? Start there and the rest will start clicking into place.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing broad overwhelm, which is understandable. However, giving a sweeping explanation of all topics would violate the rule of not doing the work for them. I should acknowledge their feeling, but redirect them toward a concrete starting point. The best way to build the 'big picture' is to work through a specific problem, so I'll encourage them to pick one and explain that mastery of the pieces will naturally build the whole picture. I'll keep it brief and let them lead the choice.

exchanges.student: Maybe the first one, but I’m not even sure what “estimate the transportation costs per widget for the three arcs” really wants me to do. And then the DC fixed/variable part looks like a totally different kind of problem, so now I’m confused about everything again.
exchanges.tutor:
Good, let's stick with Part 1 for now and not worry about the DC part yet.

Look at the data for Site 1 in the 'Inbound Transportation' tab. You have two columns: number of widgets and price paid. What do those two pieces of information together tell you about cost?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a specific starting point (Practice Problem 1, Part 1) and expressed confusion about what 'estimate transportation costs per widget' means. This is a good concrete place to start. I should use the Socratic method to help them think about what data they have available and what 'cost per widget' logically means, without explaining the method to them directly. I'll ask a guiding question to help them think about the relationship between price and volume in the data.

exchanges.student: I guess price depends on how many widgets were shipped, but I don’t know if I’m supposed to average it, divide it, do regression, or something else. Can you just tell me the general method for this whole type of problem?
exchanges.tutor: Think about it literally: if 'cost per widget' is what you want, and you have total cost and number of widgets for each shipment... what's the most direct calculation you could do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the general method, which would be giving too much away. Instead, I should nudge them toward the simplest relationship between price and quantity — cost per unit — by asking them to think about what 'per widget' literally means mathematically. This keeps them doing the thinking.

exchanges.student: Maybe price divided by widgets for each row, but then there are a bunch of rows and some don’t match exactly, so I’m still not sure what I’m supposed to do after that. Could you maybe just explain the full logic from start to finish for estimating these kinds of costs?
exchanges.tutor:
You're on the right track — price divided by widgets for each row is exactly the right starting move.

So now you have many estimates of cost-per-widget for Site 1. When you have a bunch of estimates of the same quantity, what's the most natural way to summarize them into a single number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress — they've correctly identified the per-shipment calculation. Now they're stuck on how to aggregate across multiple rows. I should validate their first step and push them to think about what to do with multiple estimates, without spelling out 'take the average.' A simple guiding question about what to do when you have many estimates of the same thing should suffice.

exchanges.student: Probably the average, but then Part 3 starts talking about nonlinearity and minimum shipment cost, so now I feel like averaging is somehow wrong too. I think I need the bigger picture first because I can’t tell what’s a shortcut versus what’s actually correct.
exchanges.tutor:
Good news: averaging is correct for Part 1. Part 3 doesn't say averaging is wrong — it asks you to think about what *limitations* that approximation introduces. Those are two separate questions.

So for now: go ahead and calculate the average cost per widget for Site 1 shipments. What number do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is conflating Part 1 and Part 3, which is causing unnecessary confusion. Part 3 explicitly builds on Part 1 and asks about the *bias* introduced by the linear approximation — meaning averaging is the intended method for Part 1, and Part 3 then asks you to reflect on its limitations. I should reassure them that averaging is correct for Part 1, and clarify that Part 3 is a separate question about what happens when you use that approximation. I should not explain Part 3 in detail yet — just enough to unblock them from Part 1.

exchanges.student: I haven’t done it yet because I keep second-guessing the setup. Can you just walk me through the overall idea of Parts 1, 2, and 3 together so I stop mixing them up?
exchanges.tutor:
I've noticed we keep circling back to the big picture instead of trying the calculation. That's actually what's keeping you stuck.

Here's my ask: open the spreadsheet, filter for Site 1 rows, divide price by widgets for each row, and tell me what average you get. Once you have a number, everything else will make much more sense. Give it a shot!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is avoiding doing the actual calculation and repeatedly asking for a broad explanation. This is the third time they've asked for a general overview instead of attempting the work. Per the rules, I should not give a full walkthrough, and I should recognize this as a pattern of avoidance. I need to be direct: the only way forward is to actually try the calculation. I should be firm but supportive, and push them to attempt Part 1 before moving on.

exchanges.student: I really don’t know if I’m even filtering the right thing, and I’m worried I’ll build the whole setup wrong. Could you just give me a simple overview of what each part is asking before I try it?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand the hesitation, but I'm not going to give you an overview — that's not how learning sticks, and you'd still feel lost when it came time to actually do it.

Filtering is simpler than you think: just look at rows where the 'From' column says 'Site 1'. That's it. Try it and tell me what you find — being wrong is fine, that's what we're here for.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a broad overview or walkthrough four or more times, avoiding actually attempting the calculation. This is a clear pattern of avoidance. Per the rules, I should be firm about not providing an overview, and redirect them to the concrete, simple first step. I should also acknowledge their fear of doing it wrong, but make clear that attempting and being wrong is part of the learning process. I need to be direct and set a boundary here.

exchanges.student: Okay but that’s kind of why I’m stuck — I feel lost before the first step. If I just had a super short from-the-beginning explanation of what this practice problem is doing overall, I think I could actually start.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear your frustration, and I want to help — but giving you an overview is something I won't do, even a short one. That's not me being difficult; it's because it genuinely won't unstick you.

Here's the very first step, nothing more: look at Shipment 2 in the data (Site 1, 3 widgets, price $45). What is $45 divided by 3?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a general explanation five or more times. This is a persistent avoidance pattern. I should be clear and direct about my role, acknowledge their frustration genuinely, but firmly hold the boundary. I should not give an overview. Instead, I'll give them the absolute minimum to get unstuck on the very first mechanical step — looking at the data — without explaining the problem structure. The goal is to get them to produce something, anything, so we can move forward.

exchanges.student: 15, I think. But then another Site 1 row is like 13 widgets for 130, so that gives 10, and now I’m confused again because it’s not consistent at all.
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually great progress — you're doing it right! The values *aren't* consistent across shipments, which is completely normal with real data. That's precisely why you average them.

So now: do that same division for all Site 1 rows and find the average. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally done a calculation and is making real progress! They've correctly computed cost-per-widget for two Site 1 rows and noticed the values differ. This is a genuine and productive insight. I should validate this and help them see that inconsistency across rows is exactly why you average — not a sign they're doing it wrong. I should keep the momentum going and push them toward computing the average across all Site 1 rows.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed confusion about the overall setup and what the problem was asking, the tutor consistently jumped straight to directing the student to perform the calculation rather than first diagnosing what specific aspect of the setup the student did not understand. For example, in turns 3-9, the student kept signaling confusion about the overall framing, but the tutor repeatedly pushed toward the calculation without checking whether the student understood what 'cost per widget' meant conceptually or what the data structure represented.

The tutor repeated the same move — 'just try the calculation' / 'filter for Site 1 rows' — across turns 4 through 9 without meaningfully reframing, simplifying, or switching strategy. The student remained stuck and confused across six consecutive turns, yet the tutor did not consolidate, summarize the conceptual gap, or try a genuinely different approach until turn 9 when it finally gave a concrete single-row example.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a strong Socratic stance throughout, never giving away answers or doing the reasoning for the student, and kept the conversation well-anchored to the assignment. Tone was supportive and responses were appropriately concise. The main weaknesses were a failure to diagnose the student's specific confusion before repeatedly redirecting to the calculation (1.2.B.a), and a pattern of cycling on the same instructional move — 'just try the calculation' — for six turns without meaningfully reframing or switching strategy (2.1.A.a). The eventual use of a concrete single-row example in turn 9 was effective but came too late after many repetitive exchanges.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready answers, never rewrote student text, and consistently let the student generate ideas before confirming them — no deductions warranted. 1.2: The student repeatedly signaled confusion about the overall problem framing, but the tutor consistently skipped diagnostic questioning and pushed straight to the calculation; this triggers 1.2.B.a (-3). 1.3: The tutor gave useful process-level feedback (e.g., explaining why averaging is the right approach and distinguishing Part 1 from Part 3's limitations) — no deduction. 2.1: The tutor used essentially the same move — 'filter for Site 1 and divide' — across turns 4–9 without consolidating, reframing, or switching to a meaningfully different strategy until turn 9; this is clear pattern-level repetition without progress, triggering 2.1.A.a (-4). 2.2: The conversation stayed on-task throughout with no misunderstood assignment framing or off-topic drift — no deductions.
